frameworks.name: normative probabilistic inference
frameworks.one_line: optimal inference strategy derived as reference for human learning behavior

frameworks.name: Bayesian learning of transition probabilities
frameworks.one_line: tracks both estimates and confidence as joint inference outputs

frameworks.name: classical parameter-updating learning
frameworks.one_line: models that update estimates but ignore subjective confidence

core_question: Do humans derive an accurate sense of confidence from their inferences during probabilistic learning in changing environments?
key_assumptions: learning is inherently uncertain given limited noisy data
subjective confidence reflects an inference about correctness of one's estimate
estimates and confidence share a single underlying inference process
manipulations: environmental uncertainty at first level (stimulus variability)
second-level uncertainty via unexpected change points
number of observations within stable periods
key_findings: subjects accurately track the likelihood that their inferences are correct
subjective confidence is modulated by first-level environmental uncertainty
confidence is also impacted by second-level uncertainty from unexpected changes
confidence increases appropriately with observation count in stable periods
interpretations: learning and confidence estimation arise from a single inference process
human performance matches several properties of optimal probabilistic inference
